existential_risks.title: The safety numbers that gate everything cannot be computed by the described stack
existential_risks.why: FS≥1.5, stall speed, range≥100km, flutter limits, and CG-stability are all hard export gates (PRD4 §4.2) and continuous Validator checks (PRD2 §1), yet no FEA solver (CalculiX/Code_Aster), mesher (Gmsh/Netgen), aero method (XFOIL/AVL/VSPAERO/SU2), or flutter solver (DLM/p-k, Nastran SOL145) is named anywhere. CL_max/stall is a viscous-separation quantity unobtainable from geometry; flutter needs a modal eigensolve coupled to unsteady aero; static margin needs a neutral-point/downwash run. The 'Structural Validator' is listed as an LLM agent, so in practice an LLM is being asked to emit FEA/flutter/FS scalars it can only hallucinate. A safety gate computed from data the system cannot produce is worse than no gate: it implies false assurance on flight hardware.
existential_risks.mitigation: Replace the LLM-Validator's numeric role with real grounded solvers: Gmsh+CalculiX for linear-static FS, a linear-buckling and modal eigensolve for stability/flutter prerequisites, AVL/VSPAERO for neutral point and induced drag, XFOIL for sectional polars, and a DLM p-k step for flutter onset. Move all of it off the interactive path. Train offline surrogates/ROMs (Kriging/GP, POD) for the live HUD with explicit error bars and validity domains, and never let an LLM originate a safety scalar — the Validator only routes solver outputs and structured failure objects.

existential_risks.title: Headline latency budgets (150ms recompile / 30Hz geometry / 15s Pareto) are off by 1–3 orders of magnitude
existential_risks.why: The product conflates cheap scalar telemetry recompute with expensive B-rep regeneration, tessellation, FEA, and slicing under one clock. A single nontrivial OCCT boolean/loft/fillet is 100ms–seconds; BRepMesh tessellation of a 2.8m aero skin to resolve 0.2mm features is 10^5–10^6 triangles and tens–hundreds of ms; a real slice (PrusaSlicer/CuraEngine CLI) of a 42h part is tens of seconds to minutes; one FEA mesh+solve is seconds–minutes; an NSGA-II/MOEA front is 10^3–10^5 evaluations. OCCT has no incremental B-rep edit, so every slider frame would re-run the feature tree. The PRD's own example contradicts itself (request 20:45:00 → cascade 20:45:02 = 2s, not 150ms). The core UX promise ('drag a slider, geometry shifts smoothly while mass/CG/print-time track live') is structurally impossible as specified.
existential_risks.mitigation: Adopt the real interactive-CAD pattern: a cheap graphical preview during drag (proxy transform or a precomputed discrete mesh family across the bounded slider range) with kernel regeneration only on release; precomputed numeric slider bounds stored in the ledger and refreshed async; analytic proxies for the 30Hz HUD (mass = volume integral, print-time = extrusion-volume/flow-rate, CG = mass-moment), with a true slice run debounced on explicit Generate-G-Code, cached by mesh hash; surrogate models for the optimization loop; LLM strictly off all hot paths.

existential_risks.title: The event-sourced ledger is non-deterministic, so replay/time-travel cannot reproduce the historical state
existential_risks.why: PRD3 §3.1 promises rollback recompiles 'the exact historical state' via the 'deterministic build123d local engine.' But the parameters being replayed are produced by non-deterministic services: the Strategic/Geometric/Validator LLMs (temperature, model-version drift), the Pareto heuristic (seed/order/IEEE-754 accumulation), and FEA/slicer outputs (tessellation tolerance, thread count). Even given identical parameters, OCCT B-rep generation is not bit- or topology-stable across kernel versions (7.5→7.8 boolean/fillet rewrites), libm/FMA/-ffast-math, or x86 vs Apple-Silicon clients. build123d also has no persistent topological naming, so a parameter change renumbers sub-shapes and silently re-binds downstream features. Replaying non-deterministic producers is the canonical event-sourcing anti-pattern; for AS9100/configuration control you cannot certify that printed part v2.4.1 matches what v2.4.1 reconstructs next quarter.
existential_risks.mitigation: Split events into replayable user-intent facts vs recomputed derivations. Persist all derived artifacts as first-class events (the exact generated build123d script, resulting STEP/B-rep + mesh, and telemetry), content-addressed by a pinned toolchain fingerprint (OCCT/build123d/Python/BLAS-threads/rounding). Never re-invoke LLM/optimizer/solver during replay — fold over stored facts only. Add a persistent-identity layer (OCAF/TNaming or feature tags baked into the generator) so feature bindings survive regeneration.

existential_risks.title: LLM-generated build123d/Python is executed with no sandbox — arbitrary RCE plus uncatchable kernel crashes
existential_risks.why: Three agents 'generate, execute, and modify' Python (PRD1 §3) with zero isolation specified. build123d sits on OCP→OpenCascade C++ via pybind11, so 'execute the script' is effectively exec() of model-authored code. A hallucination or prompt injection inside conversational design intent (a poisoned airfoil_profile string or imported .dat file) yields straight RCE: it reads ANTHROPIC/OPENAI keys from env, exfiltrates other tenants' aerospace IP, or pivots laterally. Separately, OCCT booleans/fillets on degenerate input raise C++ Standard_Failure or access violations that are NOT catchable Python exceptions — they segfault the CPython worker, and degenerate ops can spin for minutes uninterruptibly. RestrictedPython cannot contain a C extension.
existential_risks.mitigation: Run every execution in an out-of-process, pre-warmed, snapshot-restored microVM/gVisor (Firecracker/runsc) or nsjail pool with seccomp-bpf, dropped caps, RLIMIT_AS/RLIMIT_CPU, no network namespace (egress-deny), and a process-level wall-clock kill (not try/except). Pair with AST/import allow-listing. Architecturally, invert codegen: the LLM emits schema-validated parameter deltas, and a deterministic non-LLM templated layer (Jinja+build123d) produces the script — removing arbitrary code as an attack surface.

existential_risks.title: The 'single source of truth' is unenforced: LLM code is the de facto truth, the schema is decorative, and there is no validity gate
existential_risks.why: Free-form LLM Python — not the ledger JSON — actually defines geometry, so a HARD_LOCK 4.5mm pin can silently become 4.50001mm, get double-padded, or drop out, and the Validator then certifies FS against geometry that doesn't match the locked parameters. The schema cannot stop this: JSON Schema draft-2020-12 defaults additionalProperties to true (omitted everywhere), so typos fork state; the cascade writes domains.structure.knuckle_wall_thickness_mm, a node the schema never defines and that has no lock_state or bounds, so it can never be HARD_LOCKed and the weight loop can overwrite it without limit. There is no BRepCheck_Analyzer/manifold/watertight gate, so invalid or non-manifold solids flow into slicing (garbage G-code), FEA, mass/CG, and the viewport.
existential_risks.mitigation: Add a post-compile round-trip verifier: query OCC topology/bounding box and assert each ledger dimension within tolerance, plus BRepCheck_Analyzer + watertight + min-wall checks before anything reaches slicer/FEA/viewport. Harden the schema: additionalProperties:false on every object, a reusable $defs/ParameterDef ({value, unit, lock_state, bounds[min,max], precision}) referenced by every tunable node, define every node the runtime writes, and express invariants (bounds[0]≤value≤bounds[1], skin_thickness≥min-wall).

existential_risks.title: 'Pareto Frontier' optimization is misrepresented and unsafe: it is greedy single-objective fully-stressed design
existential_risks.why: No objective vector, dominance relation, non-dominated archive, or MOO algorithm exists; the flowchart is a scalar greedy descent that breaks on ΔW<0.1g — which produces one mass-minimal point, not a frontier (a frontier is a SET, measured by hypervolume/spread, not one scalar's delta). ΔW<0.1g is ~2e-5 of 4.65kg, below the mesh mass-integral noise floor, certifies neither KKT optimality nor FS feasibility, and false-converges when thinning (−ΔW) and rib-adding (+ΔW) cancel. Worse, 'thin low-stress, thicken high-stress' is fully-stressed design using a stress criterion only: thin printed skins fail by buckling, not yield, so a stress-only loop deletes exactly the skin providing buckling/flutter resistance — contradicting the Validator's own flutter check. Constraints are also treated as post-hoc gates with independent per-slider box bounds, which cannot represent the coupled feasible manifold (the PRD's own pin→wall cascade proves coupling).
existential_risks.mitigation: Specify a real MOO (NSGA-II/MOEA-D or qEHVI Bayesian) over an explicit objective vector [mass, min-FS/compliance, buckling-load-factor, print-time, L/D], evaluated through surrogates with stated accuracy. Add buckling and modal/flutter constraints, not just stress. Embed constraints inside the optimizer via constraint-domination or augmented-Lagrangian, not external gates. Use relative + KKT/feasibility convergence and hypervolume stabilization, not ΔW. Add multi-start to escape local optima.

existential_risks.title: The manufacturing strategy is internally self-contradictory and orientation-blind
existential_risks.why: PRD2 §2.2 picks single-wall spiral-vase extrusion as the headline toolpath for aero skins, but spiral-vase hard-requires exactly one perimeter, one island/layer, zero infill, zero pockets, and no topology change between layers — structurally forbidding the internal ribs/spars (PRD2 §2.1), fastener/captive-nut pockets and bulkheads (PRD1 §3.2), and lip-and-groove sectional joints (PRD1 §3.1) the rest of the platform mandates, and yielding a ~0.4mm shell with near-zero section modulus that fails FS≥1.5/flutter. Separately, '≤45° overhang' is framed as an intrinsic geometry clamp, but overhang is defined relative to a build direction the ledger has no field for; on a double-curved airfoil every orientation exceeds 45° somewhere, and clamping it would chamfer away the HARD_LOCKed airfoil_profile. After sectional splitting, each piece needs its own orientation, none stored.
existential_risks.mitigation: Drop spiral-vase for structural skins (reserve it for non-structural fairings only); use multi-perimeter shells with real internal structure. Add a build_orientation field per part and per section to the ledger and run orientation optimization (which also sets the anisotropy weak axis). Achieve supportless printing via self-supporting 45° chamfers/teardrops on holes and nut pockets, not by clamping aero surfaces. Define an arbitration rule between the manufacturing loop and locked aero geometry.

existential_risks.title: Commercial blockers ignored: AGPL slicer copyleft, LGPL local engine, ITAR/EAR, and flight-safety liability
existential_risks.why: Every production FDM slicer (PrusaSlicer/libslic3r, CuraEngine, OrcaSlicer/BambuStudio) is AGPL-3.0; the network clause (§13) means tight SaaS integration of the slicer can obligate releasing the entire proprietary server stack. OCCT is LGPL-2.1, which attaches relink/notice duties the moment the repeatedly-promised 'local engine' ships to end users. The example (1000km electric UAV airframe with cruise/flutter analysis) is plausibly ITAR USML Cat VIII / EAR-controlled, so streaming geometry+intent to a hosted LLM is a likely deemed-export — with no data-residency, on-prem/air-gapped LLM, or export gating mentioned. And asserting FS/flutter/stall verdicts on flight structures invites reasonable reliance → direct product-liability exposure if a built aircraft fails.
existential_risks.mitigation: Isolate the slicer out-of-process behind a clean network boundary, license a commercial slicer, or build a from-scratch print-time/material estimator; get IP counsel sign-off on the OCCT/build123d LGPL path before shipping a local engine. Offer an on-prem/air-gapped LLM deployment with export-control gating and per-tenant data residency, and AS9100/DFARS-grade audit. Add ToS liability limits, intended-use gating (prototype/non-flight), a licensed-PE-in-the-loop sign-off, and explicit regulatory mapping (Part 107/89, ASTM F38, CS-23/MOSAIC).

ranked_gaps.title: Safety physics (FEA/CFD/flutter/stall/range) is uncomputable by the stack and the LLM-Validator would hallucinate it
ranked_gaps.summary: No solver, mesher, aero method, or flutter solver is named; FS/stall/range/flutter gate exports on numbers the system has no mechanism to produce. The Validator is an LLM asked to emit physics scalars.

ranked_gaps.title: Latency budgets 150ms/30Hz/15s are physically impossible with real OCCT booleans + tessellation + FEA + slicing
ranked_gaps.summary: Cheap scalar recompute is conflated with expensive kernel/physics/slicer ops under one clock; off by 1–3 orders of magnitude. The 'smooth slider morph' core UX cannot ship as specified.

ranked_gaps.title: Event-sourced replay is non-deterministic (OCCT cross-version/platform + LLM + Pareto producers)
ranked_gaps.summary: Rollback recomputes non-deterministic services and OCCT is not bit/topology-stable, so 'exact historical state' is false — voiding traceability/branching/audit for aerospace.

ranked_gaps.title: Unsandboxed execution of LLM-generated Python: RCE + uncatchable OCCT segfaults
ranked_gaps.summary: exec() of model-authored code over a C++ kernel with no isolation, egress control, resource limits, or process-kill; both a security and a stability hole (one bad boolean kills the worker).

ranked_gaps.title: Ledger is not the source of truth: free-form LLM code is, HARD_LOCK is unenforced, no post-compile verification
ranked_gaps.summary: The code, not the JSON, defines geometry; locked values can drift silently and the Validator certifies geometry that doesn't match the parameters everyone believes are frozen.

ranked_gaps.title: Topological naming problem: features, picking, anchored HUD, and 'delta meshes' silently re-bind to wrong faces/edges
ranked_gaps.summary: build123d selection is positional, not identity-based; a parameter change renumbers sub-shapes so downstream pockets/joints, the clicked-face→slider map, and mesh diffing all break exactly when users iterate.

ranked_gaps.title: 'Pareto' loop is actually greedy single-objective fully-stressed design; deletes buckling/flutter-critical material
ranked_gaps.summary: No objectives/dominance/archive/algorithm; stress-only thinning ignores buckling on thin skins and contradicts the flutter check; ΔW<0.1g is unsound and not a frontier metric.

ranked_gaps.title: Schema cannot validate, bound, or lock the state the runtime writes
ranked_gaps.summary: additionalProperties defaults true; the cascade target knuckle_wall_thickness_mm is undefined and has no lock_state/bounds; no reusable ParameterDef; typos fork state silently.

ranked_gaps.title: Manufacturing self-contradiction: spiral-vase skin vs internal ribs/fasteners/joints/FS, plus orientation-blind overhang clamp
ranked_gaps.summary: The headline toolpath mode forbids all internal load-bearing structure; '45° overhang' is undefined without a build-orientation field the ledger lacks and would mutate the locked airfoil.

ranked_gaps.title: No event store, total order, IDs, or provenance; second-resolution timestamps collide at 30Hz
ranked_gaps.summary: Only two transport frames exist, not addressable persisted events; no sequence/Lamport clock, parent/causation IDs, snapshots, or coalescing — reversion/branch/merge are unimplementable.

ranked_gaps.title: AGPL slicer copyleft + LGPL local engine = commercial/legal blocker
ranked_gaps.summary: Every production slicer is AGPL-3.0 (network clause risks open-sourcing the proprietary stack); OCCT LGPL attaches when the 'local engine' ships. Must be resolved before architecture lock.

ranked_gaps.title: No auth/identity/multi-tenancy anywhere, plus unaddressed ITAR/EAR deemed-export to cloud LLMs
ranked_gaps.summary: Ledger/WebSocket carry no user/org/token; any client can read/mutate any project. Streaming controlled UAV geometry to hosted LLMs is a likely export-control violation with no on-prem option.

ranked_gaps.title: FS=1.5 single-load, isotropic, un-converged FEA used as a binary flight-safety gate = false-confidence
ranked_gaps.summary: No mesh convergence, no FDM anisotropy (Z ~30–50% weaker), no composite criterion for the carbon spar, no load envelope/knockdowns — a wrong PASS lets an under-strength airframe export.

ranked_gaps.title: WebSocket protocol missing NACK/rejection, backpressure/coalescing, reconnect/idempotency, and any mesh payload
ranked_gaps.summary: The safety floor has no wire representation; 30Hz producer vs ≥150ms consumer self-floods; no resume/dedup; and the 'delta mesh' channel/format/compression is entirely unspecified over a JSON text transport.

ranked_gaps.title: Ghost Mode needs order-independent transparency and large-assembly perf (instancing/LOD/BVH picking) that are unspecified
ranked_gaps.summary: Nested translucent shells are the degenerate case for default alpha sorting; thousands of 21700 cells/ribs blow draw-call and CPU-raycast budgets — no OIT, instancing, LOD, BVH, or library choice.

themes: Single-clock fallacy: cheap scalar recompute (mass/CG telemetry) is conflated with expensive B-rep regeneration, tessellation, FEA, and slicing, all promised at 150ms/30Hz/15s. Every performance claim assumes the slowest tool runs at the speed of the fastest.

Non-determinism poisoning an event-sourced foundation: OCCT cross-version/platform drift, LLM stochasticity, and heuristic-optimizer path-dependence make replay irreproducible, which silently invalidates traceability, branching, merge, and aerospace certification.

LLM placed inside hard real-time and safety-critical loops it categorically cannot serve — it is too slow for 150ms/30Hz and cannot originate FEA/flutter/FS numbers without hallucinating; the architecture must push it to the human-timescale conversational turn only.

'Single source of truth' asserted but unenforced at every layer: the schema (addit.Properties, undefined nodes), the codegen (free-form LLM Python becomes the real truth), and HARD_LOCK (no verification) are all decorative rather than load-bearing.

Safety theater: FS≥1.5, flutter, stall, range, and CG-stability are presented as hard gates while the stack contains no mechanism to compute any of them — a verdict from absent data is more dangerous than an honest 'unknown.'

Topological naming is a single root cause recurring across five lenses (kernel mutation, picking/anchored-HUD, delta-mesh diffing, optimization feature targeting, merge), yet no persistent-identity layer is specified.

The entire 'boring' production substrate is absent: sandbox, auth/multi-tenancy, persistence/event store, job queue/backpressure, secrets, cost-per-design model, observability/audit, and legal/IP — each independently fatal for a commercial aerospace tool.

Geometry-kernel reality is treated as a one-liner: shelling, offset of curved closed profiles, lip-and-groove mating, sliver/feather edges, and validity repair (BRepCheck/ShapeFix) are OCCT's least-robust operations and have no fallback path.
still_missing: No assembly/kinematics/mates model. The landing-gear 'pivot pin' implies a deployable mechanism with DOF and motion, and CG + clearance differ gear-up vs gear-down, but the ledger has no joints, mates, motion study, or multi-pose representation — so 'clearance envelope' checks and CG are single-pose only and the mechanism cannot be validated for retraction interference.

No COTS component database or BOM-driven mass/CG model. The Mass=4.65kg and CG=34.2% MAC telemetry depend on the carbon spar, 21700 cells, motors, props, ESCs, wiring, fasteners, and inserts — none of which have masses/densities/CG offsets anywhere. Mass is implicitly treated as printed-volume only, so the headline mass/CG/range figures are unbacked.

No propulsion/energy/aero-performance model behind the 1000km range and 'high-altitude cruise strategy' claims. The Strategic agent 'maps airspeed profiles and cruise strategy' with no motor/prop efficiency map, battery Wh/specific-energy, L/D, or Breguet-electric energy balance — the mission target the whole design optimizes toward is unmodeled.

No thermal domain at all, despite a printed-polymer 'high-altitude cruise' airframe. Missing: FDM polymer glass transition (~55–60°C PLA) vs solar/aero heating, high-altitude cold/UV embrittlement, and 21700 battery thermal management/runaway — thermal is often the binding constraint for electric high-altitude UAVs.

No requirements-traceability / verification matrix. Nothing links top-level mission requirements (range, stall, FS) to the parameters and analyses that satisfy them, or re-flags downstream requirements when an upstream HARD_LOCK changes — a core systems-engineering artifact for the aerospace framing.

No post-processing / assembly output. Sectional splitting yields many parts a human must assemble, but there is no generated assembly sequence, part numbering, fastener/insert BOM, pin-keying scheme, or multi-plate orientation packing across the printer fleet — each split section also needs its own (unmodeled) build orientation.

No empirical calibration loop. The +0.2mm slip-fit padding, anisotropic shrinkage compensation, FDM knockdown factors, and any surrogate models all require measured as-built data to be trustworthy, but there is no feedback path from physical prints/tests back into the model parameters — the tolerances are asserted constants.

No neutral CAD interchange or inspection output. Only 'Generate G-Code' exists; there is no STEP/3MF/IGES export, no engineering drawing, and no GD&T/PMI (ASME Y14.5 / ISO GPS) — so parts cannot be dimensionally inspected, accepted, or handed to any downstream CAM/QA process.

No conversational ambiguity/disambiguation protocol or failure/degraded-mode UX. There is no defined behavior for contradictory or under-specified natural-language intent, no confidence surfacing, and no UX for what the user sees when generation, a solver, or the sandbox kill fails mid-design.

No assembly/mate/kinematics model: the landing-gear pivot and flight control surfaces are mechanisms, but the system represents only static solids — no joints, DOF, motion ranges, or swept-volume interference checking: The entire architecture treats every component as a static B-rep solid in a fixed pose, yet the named domain is full of mechanisms. A 'front_pivot_pin' (PRD4 1) and 'landing gear knuckle' (PRD4 2.2) is a revolute joint — retractable gear rotates through a stowage arc; a flight vehicle has ailerons/elevator/rudder control surfaces that hinge. build123d/OCCT has no constraint/mate solver and no degrees-of-freedom representation (build123d's Joint helpers position parts but do not solve a kinematic network or simulate motion). PRD1 2.2 claims a landing-gear bracket mutation 'validates clearance envelope constraints,' but the relevant check for a moving gear leg is a SWEPT-VOLUME interference test through the full retraction arc (does the retracted wheel collide with the spar, battery bay, or skin at every angle?), not a static clearance check. Nothing in the ledger encodes joint axes, motion limits, gear-up vs gear-down states, or control-surface deflection range. Consequences: (1) no interference-under-motion analysis, so a 'valid' static model can be a mechanism that jams or collides mid-travel; (2) the pivot pin's structural load case is a bearing/shear joint reaction the monolithic FEA can't see without joint reactions; (3) CG and the export gate are computed for one frozen pose, ignoring that gear-up vs gear-down shifts CG (a flight-safety quantity). This is a whole missing subsystem (kinematic/assembly modeling), not a parameter.

No Bill-of-Materials or non-structural component mass catalog — the CG/Mass HUD and the export-blocking CG%MAC guardrail are physically uncomputable because the masses that dominate CG (batteries, motors, ESCs, servos, payload, fasteners, spar) are absent from the schema: PRD3 2.5 / PRD4 2.2 surface Mass=4.65kg and CG=34.2% MAC as live, safety-relevant readouts, and PRD4 4.2 blocks export on CG/stability. But on a real electric UAV the CG is dominated by concentrated non-structural masses, almost none of which exist in the ledger. The schema (PRD4 1) has cell_type/configuration/total_cells but NO cell mass, and NO motor, propeller, ESC, servo, avionics/autopilot, wiring harness, payload, or fastener masses or positions — and material_profile is a bare string with no density, so even the printed-structure mass can't be integrated. A 21700 cell is ~70g; dozens-to-hundreds of them plus motors are >70% of vehicle mass and set the CG location. Without a positioned BOM (mass + centroid + mounting location per item) you cannot compute total mass, the mass-moment integral for CG, or the mass distribution the flutter/modal analysis needs. The 'Mass Budget Matrix' breakdown into 'structural, payload, and power storage' (PRD3 2.5) has no payload or power-mass data to break down. So the headline telemetry and a hard export gate are driven by numbers the data model cannot produce; they can only be fabricated. This is distinct from the 'as-printed mass' note in the structural lens — the gap is the total absence of a non-geometric component/mass library.

No propulsion/energy/mission-analysis subsystem and no battery electrical/thermal model — the '1000km range, high-altitude cruise' goal and the range>=100km export gate have no computational basis, and stratospheric battery derating is ignored: PRD2 1 has the Strategic agent 'determine the necessary high-altitude cruise strategy' for a '1000km range' and PRD4 4.2 blocks export when 'range drops below 100km,' yet no subsystem computes propulsion or energy. Missing entirely: a propulsion model (motor efficiency map, propeller/ducted-fan thrust and efficiency vs airspeed/altitude, ESC losses), a power-required model (P_req = drag*V / (eta_prop*eta_motor) at cruise), and a battery energy model (usable Wh = cells * capacity * voltage * depth-of-discharge, minus reserves). Range for an electric aircraft is E_battery*eta_total*(L/D)/(g*m) — it needs L/D (no aero solver, per existing gaps) AND the propulsion/battery chain that is wholly absent. Worse for the stated 'high-altitude cruise': at stratospheric -40 to -56C, Li-ion 21700 cells lose large fractions of usable capacity and max discharge rate, and reduced air density raises required CL/airspeed and cuts propeller efficiency — all of which slash range. There is no thermal/altitude derating, no battery pack thermal model, and no reserve/end-of-life knockdown. A flight-critical range gate is therefore asserted with no propulsion physics behind it.

Material is a string with no property database, and there is no operating-environment thermal model — FEA constants, thermal-shrinkage compensation, CTE mismatch (carbon spar vs plastic), and Tg/cold-brittleness margins are all unavailable: material_profile and cell_type are bare strings (PRD4 1) with no linked property record. FEA cannot run without E, nu, rho, yield/UTS; thermal-shrinkage padding (PRD1 3.2) needs per-material, anisotropic, dimension-scaling shrinkage (PLA ~0.3%, ABS ~0.7-0.8%, nylon higher) yet the spec hardcodes a constant +0.2mm absolute offset that is wrong on a 2800mm part (shrinkage is a percentage, so absolute error grows with size); slip-fit on a 16mm carbon spar needs both mating materials' properties. No material-property database (queryable allowables, temperature-dependent curves) exists. Separately and more deeply, there is NO operating-environment thermal model at all: at high-altitude cruise the airframe sits near/below several thermoplastics' ductile-brittle and Tg-relevant range (PLA Tg ~55-60C is the hot-side risk on a black wing in sun on the ground; cold-side embrittlement is the cruise risk), and the carbon-fiber spar (CTE ~ -0.5 to 2 ppm/K axially) bonded/slip-fit into a plastic structure (CTE ~50-90 ppm/K) develops large thermal strains over a 100K ground-to-cruise swing — loosening or binding the +0.2mm slip-fit and inducing thermal stress the isothermal FEA never computes. Thermal is a named hint domain and is absent from all four PRDs.

No requirements model or verification/traceability matrix — the 'automated systems engineer' cannot link goals to parameters to verification, so it cannot show a requirement is met or what a weight-shaving change violated: PRD1 1/PRD2 1 brand the system an 'automated systems engineer' translating goals (1000km range, FS>=1.5) into a design, but there is no requirements artifact anywhere: no requirement IDs, no decomposition of top-level goals into derived/allocated requirements, no allocation of each requirement to the parameters/features that satisfy it, and no verification matrix (analysis/test method, status, margin per requirement). Real systems engineering for flight hardware (ARP4754A/AS9100 expectations) is built on requirement->design->verification bidirectional traceability. Concrete failures from its absence: (1) when the Pareto loop thins material, nothing identifies WHICH requirement bounds the thinning, so the loop can silently trade away an unstated requirement; (2) the 'Aerospace Horizon' red gate fires on range/stall but there's no record of the originating requirement, its rationale, or acceptable margin; (3) reversion/branch-compare (PRD3 3) compares parameter values but cannot answer 'which branch satisfies more requirements,' because requirements aren't modeled; (4) no acceptance criteria per requirement means 'manufacturable/airworthy' is unfalsifiable. The event ledger records parameter deltas but no requirement-satisfaction state, so traceability — the backbone of the claimed role — is structurally missing.

No coordinate-frame/datum management — multi-section reassembly, alignment-pin mating, and CG%MAC all require shared datums and an MAC reference that the ledger never defines: The system slices a 2.8m wing into 200mm sections (PRD1 3.1), gives each its own print orientation (per existing manufacturing lens), and must reassemble them — but there is no global coordinate frame, no datum/origin model, and no per-part local-frame-to-assembly-frame transform anywhere in the schema. Three concrete breakages: (1) Alignment pins on section A must register to holes on section B within a SHARED datum reference frame; with only positional/index selectors and no datum system, the two parts' features are defined in independent local frames with no guaranteed registration, so 'straight assembly alignment' has no mathematical basis. (2) CG=34.2% MAC (PRD3 HUD, PRD4 4.2) is meaningless without a defined Mean Aerodynamic Chord geometry, its spanwise location, and the wing leading-edge datum from which %MAC is measured — none are in the ledger; %MAC also requires every mass to be expressed in one consistent body frame (compounding the missing-BOM gap). (3) Stability (neutral point, static margin) and aero are measured in stability/body axes that must be tied to the same datum as the mass model. Datums and reference frames are foundational CAD/aero infrastructure the architecture omits entirely.

The structural model is monolithic but the manufactured article is a bonded/pinned assembly of 14+ printed sections — joint bond-line and pin load transfer are never analyzed, so any FS is computed on a structure that doesn't physically exist: PRD1 3.1 splits large components into sections joined by printed lip-and-groove edges and alignment pins, i.e. the as-built wing is many short FDM segments bonded/mechanically joined end-to-end. But the FEA/FS logic (PRD2 1/2.1, PRD4 4.2) operates on the continuous generated solid as if monolithic. The segmentation introduces the actual failure-initiation sites the analysis ignores: (1) each lip-and-groove interface is a bonded/adhesive or interference joint whose strength is governed by bond-line area, adhesive shear/peel allowables, and the FDM layer orientation at the joint — not the parent material; the bond line is typically the weakest link in spanwise bending, and nothing models it as a contact/cohesive interface. (2) Alignment pins become shear-loaded fasteners transferring bending across the cut; their bearing/shear and the surrounding printed boss must be sized, but they're described as mere assembly aids. (3) The validator's FS>=1.5 is therefore evaluated against a continuous beam, while the real article fails at the discrete joints far below that — a dangerous non-conservative error. A correct model needs a segmented assembly FE model with cohesive/contact joints and pin reactions; the architecture has neither the assembly representation nor the joint-strength data to build it.

No human-in-the-loop review gate: AI cascades, weight-shaving, and auto-merge silently mutate flight geometry with no preview/diff, no granular accept/reject, and no intent-disambiguation protocol: The system continuously and automatically rewrites geometry — cascades (PRD4 2.2 silently thickens a knuckle wall), the weight loop thins/ribs (PRD2 2.1), and merge 'programmatically alters mounting holes or clearance paths' (PRD3 3.3) — yet the only user control is HARD_LOCK. Missing human-in-the-loop machinery for a tool producing flight hardware: (1) no change-preview/diff step letting the engineer see and approve what the AI changed BEFORE it commits (the cascade is applied, then reported); (2) no granular accept/reject — a user cannot reject one cascade while keeping others, nor reject an auto-merge edit, so the only options are accept-all or HARD_LOCK-everything; (3) no commit/sign-off/approval state distinguishing 'AI-proposed' from 'engineer-reviewed' (which the missing requirements-traceability and audit needs depend on); (4) no conversational intent disambiguation — natural language ('make the wing stronger,' '6S,' '2 inch') is ambiguous in objective, units, and scope, but there is no clarification loop, no confirmation of the parsed intent, and no units-in-speech handling, so an LLM misread silently becomes a HARD_LOCK or geometry change. For safety-relevant output, the absence of an explicit review/approval boundary is both a usability and a liability hole.

No tolerance stack-up analysis across the segmented assembly — per-joint +/-0.2mm fits and pin clearances accumulate over 14+ sections into gross misalignment/twist that corrupts the very airfoil the aero analysis assumes: Each sectional joint carries a +0.2mm slip-fit (PRD1 3.2) plus alignment-pin clearance, and FDM holds only ~+/-0.1-0.5mm per feature. A 2.8m wing at 200mm increments is ~14 joints per side; cumulative tolerance stack-up over a serial chain of 14 clearance-fit interfaces accumulates both translational error and, more damagingly, angular error that compounds along the span — a small per-joint angular slop integrates into centimeters of tip displacement and degrees of unintended washout/twist. That directly changes the spanwise twist distribution, hence the lift distribution, stall behavior, and the CG/aero numbers the export gate trusts, and can preload the structure. There is no tolerance-budget/stack-up analysis (worst-case or RSS), no GD&T datum scheme to control accumulation (ties to the missing-datum gap), and no allocation of a per-joint tolerance from an assembly-level requirement. The slip-fit clearance is treated as a local per-feature constant with no awareness that clearances in series destroy assembly-level geometric conformance.

External datasets the build depends on (component/material database, thread-spec tables, airfoil coordinate files) are unversioned dependencies outside the event-sourced ledger — breaking reproducibility, traceability, and supply-chain integrity: PRD1 3.2 references a 'target component database' for tolerance modifiers and 'standardized thread specifications' for fastener pockets; material_profile/cell_type/airfoil_profile (PRD4 1) are string keys that must resolve against external tables (material properties, cell specs, airfoil .dat coordinate sets). None of this referenced data lives in the ledger, and none is version-pinned in the event stream. Consequences orthogonal to the kernel/LLM determinism already flagged: (1) Reproducibility — re-running a historical commit after the material DB, thread table, or airfoil definition is edited yields different geometry/mass even with byte-identical parameters and a pinned kernel; the 'exact historical state' (PRD3 3.1) silently changes. (2) Traceability/qualification — an aerospace audit must know exactly which material allowable, thread spec, and airfoil coordinate revision produced a given commit; the ledger records the key but not the resolved value or its source revision. (3) Supply-chain integrity — these external tables (and any pip-resolved build123d/OCP versions) are an unmanaged trust and configuration boundary. The event log must capture content-hashes/versions of every external dataset consumed, or it is not a faithful record.

judge_scores.lens: Technical feasibility & risk: will the chosen kernel/physics/sandbox actually work, and does the plan honestly handle the existential risks — that an LLM+FEA cannot live in the 150ms hot path, and that executing model-generated code must be sandboxed against uncatchable OCCT failures and RCE?

scores.approach_name: Anvil — A Polyglot Performance-Core Architecture for Grounded Text-to-CAD
scores.rationale: Most technically defensible answer to the literal lens question of whether the kernel will work. It is the only plan that confronts build123d's real fatal flaw — it has no persistent topological naming, so feature tags renumber on regen and slider/picking/HUD bindings rot — by dropping to raw OCCT 7.8 in C++ where OCAF+TNaming genuinely exist, rather than bolting a heuristic 'sidecar TNaming-style map' onto OCP (which the other two do and which is fragile because OCP's pybind11 surface for OCAF documents is incomplete). The Manifold-library-as-preview-proxy / OCCT-as-authoritative split is the single smartest geometry idea across all three: Manifold gives guaranteed-watertight, fast, deterministic booleans for the drag proxy while OCCT booleans/fillets/shelling (which routinely throw Standard_Failure on slivers and offset of closed curved profiles) are reserved for authoritative regen behind BRepCheck/ShapeFix. Sandbox story is correct and load-bearing: Firecracker snapshot-restore pool, egress-deny netns, RLIMIT_AS/CPU, host-side wall-clock SIGKILL — and it correctly states WHY try/except is insufficient (degenerate OCCT booleans spin uninterruptibly and access violations segfault CPython). Most honest plan on the clock: it explicitly reclassifies 150ms as achievable ONLY for an isolated local feature branch and concedes multi-feature/global edits cannot hit it on OCCT — that is the technically true statement. Honest 9-12 month timeline. The real risk it carries, and acknowledges, is integration: a Rust+C+++Python+TS polyglot with a cxx/OCCT FFI bridge (opencascade-rs is alpha-grade) plus a dual-kernel Manifold/OCCT consistency surface (two kernels can disagree on a boolean, so the proxy can mislead vs the authoritative result). That execution risk is what keeps it at 8 rather than 9.
scores.strongest_idea: Manifold (guaranteed-watertight, fast, deterministic) as the live preview/proxy kernel while OCCT remains authoritative behind BRepCheck/ShapeFix, combined with real OCAF/TNaming persistent naming from C++ OCCT — directly solving build123d's missing-stable-identity problem that the other two paper over.
scores.weakest_point: The polyglot core (Rust shelling OCCT via cxx, plus Python codegen, plus Go-less but still multi-runtime) and the immature opencascade-rs/cxx FFI boundary are a genuine build-risk; and running two geometry kernels (Manifold preview vs OCCT truth) introduces a silent divergence surface where the drag proxy can show geometry the authoritative regen won't reproduce.

scores.approach_name: Grounded Monolith: A Python-First Co-Modeling Core with an Out-of-Band Truth Plane
scores.rationale: Lowest integration risk and the most vivid, correct articulation of the safety thesis, which is exactly what the lens rewards on the sandbox axis. One FastAPI process tree, Postgres as both event store and job-state store, Redis, MinIO, one deployable image — minimum moving parts is itself a feasibility advantage because integration complexity is what actually kills hardware-adjacent MVPs. Its compute-isolation paragraph is the strongest of the three in prose: it names that OCCT raises C++ Standard_Failure and access violations that segfault CPython and that degenerate booleans spin uninterruptibly, that a parent wall-clock kill (not try/except) is mandatory, and that RestrictedPython is categorically insufficient against C-extension crash/RCE — only a microVM contains the residual risk. The three core inversions are all present and correct (LLM emits Pydantic-validated deltas not free Python; deterministic Jinja2+build123d templating; grounded CalculiX/AVL/XFOIL/p-k DLM off-path; surrogate HUD with explicit error bars and 'unknown' outside validity domain). The post-compile OCC round-trip verifier (assert HARD_LOCK 4.5mm did not drift to 4.50001mm) and the BLAS-thread-count/rounding-mode pin for byte-identical replay are the most rigorous determinism story. Two real dings against the lens: (1) Timeline honesty — claiming a credible MVP in 12-16 weeks that INCLUDES Gmsh+CalculiX FS/buckling/modal, AVL/XFOIL, p-k flutter, segmented-assembly joint FEA, surrogate training, and NSGA-II is not credible; the other two correctly call the 12-week plan a fantasy and say 9-12 months, so Plan 1's self-assurance that 'inverted codegen makes this timeline real' is the one place it papers over risk. (2) Its persistent-identity mechanism — a heuristic OCAF/TNaming-style sidecar map layered on build123d/OCP — is the weakest naming approach of the three (OCP doesn't cleanly expose TNaming; face-labeling by geometric heuristic is brittle across booleans). Plus Postgres-as-event-store won't carry heavy concurrent solver load, which it concedes.
scores.strongest_idea: The two-plane framing executed as a true minimum-moving-parts monolith with a warm Firecracker/gVisor pool: the interactive plane does only closed-form analytic work (mass=Σρ·V sensitivities, mass-moment CG, extrusion-volume print-time) while the truth plane (regen/FEA/slice/optimize) is content-addressed and never blocks a frame — the lowest-integration-risk way to honor the clock separation.
scores.weakest_point: 12-16 week 'credible MVP' including flutter + segmented joint FEA + surrogate training is timeline optimism the lens should penalize, and the build123d-sidecar persistent-naming scheme is the most fragile stable-identity mechanism of the three.

scores.approach_name: Str( CloudForge ): A Cloud-Native, Event-Driven Co-Modeling Substrate with a Grounded-Solver Safety Plane
scores.rationale: Identical and correct on the three existential inversions (LLM emits schema-validated deltas only and never originates a safety scalar; deterministic Jinja2+build123d codegen; grounded CalculiX/AVL/VSPAERO/XFOIL/DLM off the interactive path; Firecracker pool with egress-deny, seccomp, RLIMIT, host-side SIGKILL and the correct rationale about uncatchable OCCT failures), and it honestly reclassifies 150ms as preview-on-drag / regen-on-release. The Go hot gateway is a reasonable, load-bearing choice for 30Hz fan-out. But on a lens that prizes risk minimization, this plan adds the most NON-load-bearing complexity and therefore the most failure surface for no proportional feasibility gain: a Yjs/Automerge CRDT collaborative ledger layered over flight geometry (it then has to downgrade semantic auto-merge to AI-proposed-with-human-review anyway, conceding the CRDT semantics don't safely apply to geometry — so the CRDT is mostly risk without payoff), EventStoreDB 'or AWS Kinesis+DynamoDB,' WebTransport 'or WebSocket,' pgvector, KEDA, Temporal, Aurora Serverless, NATS — the most managed-service sprawl and the least decisive ('or' choices in the datastore/transport/event-store layers undercut the lens's demand for a chosen, committed substrate). Its persistent-naming claim (OCAF+TNaming 'via build123d/OCP') is the same fragile approach as Plan 1 but stacked on far more moving parts, and unlike Plan 2 it never resolves the build123d-has-no-stable-identity problem at the kernel level. Honest 9-12 month timeline and good legal/ITAR posture, but the operational risk profile is the heaviest of the three and several pieces (CRDT over airframe geometry, multi-option infra) read as resume-driven rather than feasibility-driven.
scores.strongest_idea: Splitting one mythical clock into three explicitly tool-bound tiers (16-33ms analytic HUD in Go / OCCT kernel-regen on release / minutes-scale solver+Pareto DAG on Temporal) with constraint-domination embedding buckling+flutter feasibility inside the NSGA-II/qEHVI optimizer rather than as post-hoc gates.
scores.weakest_point: Heaviest, least-decisive infrastructure (EventStoreDB-or-Kinesis, WebTransport-or-WS, Aurora+NATS+KEDA+Temporal+pgvector) plus a Yjs/Automerge CRDT over flight geometry that it must immediately neuter into 'AI-proposed + mandatory human review' — added risk surface without a corresponding feasibility return, and it still inherits the fragile OCP-based persistent-naming gap without solving it.

judge_scores.lens: Time-to-a-credible-MVP and team/operational simplicity: fewest moving parts, fastest demoable end-to-end slice, hiring/skill realism.

scores.approach_name: Grounded Monolith: A Python-First Co-Modeling Core with an Out-of-Band Truth Plane
scores.rationale: On this lens it is the clear winner. It is the only plan that exploits the one structural fact that collapses moving parts: build123d/OCP is Python, so kernel, Jinja2 codegen, analytic telemetry, and solver drivers all live in one FastAPI image and one repo. The supporting cast is deliberately minimal and each piece is a single-purpose commodity: Postgres doubles as event store AND job-state store (no Kafka/EventStoreDB), Redis is the only broker (Dramatiq, not Temporal/NATS/Celery+RabbitMQ), MinIO is the only blob store, and the MVP ships as one docker-compose on a single Linux VM. The hiring profile is the most realistic of the three: Python backend + React/r3f frontend devs are abundant; the only specialist gap is the Firecracker/microVM pool, and they explicitly offer gVisor/runsc as the lighter fallback. The re-sequenced 12-16 week plan is the most aggressive on paper but also the most internally honest: the demoable end-to-end slice (morph-target proxy preview + 30Hz closed-form HUD + conversational parameter deltas + warm-pool kernel regen) is reachable early, with real FEA/flutter/optimizer correctly pushed to weeks 10-16 where slippage hurts the demo least. Two planes in one process is the minimum viable topology for a credible safety story. Not a 9/10 because real ops complexity remains: a warm Firecracker pool with snapshot-restore, plus five vendored solver binaries (Gmsh, CalculiX, AVL, XFOIL, PrusaSlicer) to package, pin, and wire, means 'fewest moving parts' is relative, not absolute, and the 12-week floor still requires the FEA/flutter layer to be demo-stubbed initially.
scores.strongest_idea: Collapsing the event store and job-state store into the single Postgres instance and using one FastAPI deployable image — Python-everywhere because the kernel is Python — removes an entire category of cross-service plumbing, schema drift, and infra to staff, which is exactly what shortens time-to-MVP.
scores.weakest_point: The 12-16 week estimate is still optimistic for anything beyond the proxy-preview demo: vendoring and wiring 5 solver binaries plus a snapshot-restored Firecracker pool plus the OCAF/TNaming persistent-tag layer is real specialist work, so the genuinely credible (solver-backed) MVP likely lands closer to the upper bound and the Firecracker piece is the one hard-to-hire dependency.

scores.approach_name: Str( CloudForge ): A Cloud-Native, Event-Driven Co-Modeling Substrate with a Grounded-Solver Safety Plane
scores.rationale: Architecturally sound and it makes the same correct inversions as Plan 1 (LLM emits deltas not code, three honest clocks, solvers off-path), but on the simplicity/time lens it carries materially more moving parts and a longer hiring list for no MVP-stage payoff. It is two languages (Go gateway + Python compute) plus a stack of distinct managed systems that each need expertise: EventStoreDB (or Kinesis+DynamoDB streams), Temporal, Aurora Serverless v2 with RLS, ElastiCache, S3, EKS + KEDA, Firecracker on a bare node pool, pgvector, LangGraph, and Yjs/Automerge CRDTs. The Go gateway is the weakest justification at MVP scale — Python/asyncio or even the FastAPI WebSocket can serve a 30Hz analytic HUD for a demo without a second language and a second deploy artifact; the GC-predictability argument is a scale concern, not a demo concern. CRDT auto-merge of flight geometry and EventStoreDB streaming projections are real-collaboration/scale features being pulled forward into the foundation. Their own estimate is 9-12 months, and the only fast item — a clickable proxy-preview demo in ~6 weeks — is explicitly labeled non-engineering. The hiring reality (Go + Python + EKS/KEDA + Temporal + EventStoreDB + CRDT + Firecracker in one team) is a meaningfully harder staffing problem than Plan 1's Python+React core.
scores.strongest_idea: Re-tiering the WebSocket protocol into PARAM_MUTATION / PREVIEW_DELTA(30Hz) / KERNEL_REGEN_COMPLETE / SOLVER_RESULT with correlation IDs is a clean, honest contract that makes the three-clock separation explicit on the wire and is genuinely demoable.
scores.weakest_point: Pulling EventStoreDB, Temporal, CRDT auto-merge, a separate Go gateway, and full EKS+KEDA into the MVP foundation multiplies infrastructure and the hiring matrix without buying anything the demo needs — the same end-to-end slice is reachable far sooner on a Python monolith, so this front-loads scale/collaboration cost the MVP does not require.

scores.approach_name: Anvil — A Polyglot Performance-Core Architecture for Grounded Text-to-CAD
scores.rationale: The technical reasoning is the most rigorous of the three (Manifold for fast watertight booleans vs OCCT for authoritative regen, OCAF/TNaming naming, segmented-assembly cohesive joints), but it is the direct antithesis of this lens. The defining choice — a compiled Rust/C++ performance core wrapping OCCT 7.8 through a cxx FFI bridge — is premature optimization that maximizes both time-to-MVP and hiring difficulty. OCCT regen latency at MVP is dominated by the kernel's own boolean/fillet cost, not by Python orchestration overhead, so Rust buys almost nothing for a demo while the cxx/OCCT FFI boundary (they admit it is 'fiddly') is a notorious time sink, and engineers who can do Rust + OpenCASCADE C++ FFI simultaneously are a rare and expensive hire. On top of that it is the heaviest topology: six service planes, four languages (Rust+C+++Python+TS), WebTransport/HTTP-3 over QUIC (with WebSocket fallback you then maintain twice), Temporal + NATS + Vault, Kubernetes on AWS GovCloud, and a bare-metal node pool for Firecracker. Every one of those is an additional system to operate and staff. Their own estimate is 9-12 months, and unlike Plan 1 the front-loaded complexity is in the foundation (the Rust core), so there is no fast early demoable slice — the proxy preview still has to come up the cxx/Manifold/OCCT stack. It avoids a 1-2 only because the design is coherent and correctly defers solvers off-path; it earns no more because nothing here serves speed or simplicity.
scores.strongest_idea: Using the Manifold library for guaranteed-watertight live-preview booleans while reserving OCCT for authoritative regen is a genuinely smart kernel split — but it is a performance/robustness refinement that belongs well after an MVP, not a foundation decision.
scores.weakest_point: Building a Rust performance core over OCCT via the cxx FFI bridge as the MVP foundation adds the rarest hire on the market and the project's most fragile integration boundary to buy latency the demo does not need, making it the slowest and most operationally complex of the three by a wide margin.

judge_scores.lens: Long-term scalability, multi-tenancy, durability, collaborative versioning (event sourcing + merge), and cost-at-scale economics. All three plans inherit the same sound event-sourcing core (replayable user-intent FACTS vs. recomputed DERIVATIONS, content-addressed S3/MinIO artifacts keyed by a pinned toolchain fingerprint + external-dataset content-hashes, replay that never re-invokes a non-deterministic producer). That core is durability-strong and identical across all three, so it is not a differentiator. The differentiators on this lens are: (a) whether the architecture actually scales out horizontally and elastically vs. deferring scale to 'later'; (b) whether multi-tenancy is a first-class isolation model (RLS / namespace) or merely an on-prem-per-customer dodge; (c) whether the 'collaborative versioning' half of the lens — concurrent multi-user editing and semantic MERGE (PRD3 §3.3) — is actually built or punted to human-reviewed diffs; (d) durable long-running job orchestration (Temporal-class resumability vs. a plain broker); and (e) cost trajectory at scale (idle/fixed cost vs. scale-to-zero elasticity, and per-unit compute efficiency). On the merge dimension specifically, two of the three plans effectively skip it.

scores.approach_name: Grounded Monolith: A Python-First Co-Modeling Core with an Out-of-Band Truth Plane
scores.rationale: On a lens that is entirely about scale, this plan's organizing philosophy ('minimum moving parts, one deployable image') works against it, and it says so out loud: tradeoff #2 concedes the 'monolith + Postgres-as-event-store will not scale to thousands of concurrent heavy jobs without later splitting the worker tier and possibly moving to a dedicated event log.' SCALABILITY: a single FastAPI image with Dramatiq/Redis workers does separate the heavy tier, but Postgres is simultaneously the system of record, the append-only event store, AND the job-state store — that triple duty is the contention bottleneck and the single point of write-amplification that caps throughput; there is no described autoscaling-on-queue-depth, no scale-to-zero, no separate node pools beyond 'later K8s.' MULTI-TENANCY: the weakest of the three — there is no row-level security, no per-tenant namespace, no tenant-scoped storage; isolation is reduced to two deployment SKUs (hosted vs. on-prem/air-gapped 'per-tenant data residency'), i.e. one-container-per-customer, which is the most expensive and least dense way to run a SaaS and does nothing for many-small-tenants economics. COLLABORATIVE VERSIONING: event sourcing is solid (per-project sequence, optimistic concurrency, branch ops as events) but the MERGE half of the lens is absent — concurrent editors collide on the single per-project sequence number and one must retry; PRD3 §3.3 semantic merge is handled only as a human-reviewed accept/reject diff, with no CRDT or real-time co-editing substrate. DURABILITY: genuinely good (content-addressed, fingerprinted, pure-fold replay), but Dramatiq is a retrying task queue, not a durable workflow engine, so a multi-step solver/optimization DAG that fails mid-run has no resumable saga state the way a Temporal-based plan does, and the single Postgres is the lone durable store. COST-AT-SCALE: cheapest to stand up and cheapest at low volume, but the trajectory is the worst — no elasticity means idle Firecracker warm pools (admitted, tradeoff #4) and an always-on monolith are paid for continuously, and the Postgres-does-everything design forces a costly re-architecture exactly when load arrives. Per-design token/cost accounting as events is a genuine plus for cost observability. Net: durability-good, everything-else-deferred; an honest MVP that is explicitly not built for the things this lens grades.
scores.strongest_idea: Splitting persisted events into replayable user-intent FACTS vs. recomputed DERIVATIONS, each carrying a full toolchain fingerprint (OCCT/build123d/Python/BLAS-thread-count/rounding) and external-dataset content-hashes, so replay is a pure fold that never re-invokes the LLM/optimizer/solver — byte-identical AS9100 reconstruction without coupling durability to a non-deterministic producer.
scores.weakest_point: It self-certifies that the monolith and Postgres-as-event-store cannot scale and punts both true multi-tenant isolation (no RLS/namespace; only on-prem-per-customer) and the entire collaborative-merge requirement (human diff only, no CRDT) — three of the five lens dimensions are deliberately deferred rather than designed.

scores.approach_name: Anvil — A Polyglot Performance-Core Architecture for Grounded Text-to-CAD
scores.rationale: Strong on the infrastructure dimensions of the lens, with one real gap. SCALABILITY: the cleanest horizontal decomposition of the three — six independent service planes (Rust/axum gateway, watchdogged OCCT core, Python AI/codegen, solver farm, slicer sidecar, stores) that scale independently; Temporal provides durable, backpressured, retry/timeout/cancellable orchestration for the long solver and optimization DAGs (a meaningful durability+scale upgrade over a plain broker), and NATS gives low-latency fan-out. The Rust core is the best per-unit compute story on the planet's hot path (schema validation, telemetry, surrogate eval at a fraction of Python's CPU/RAM), which is the single biggest lever on cost-per-frame at 30Hz and cost-per-regen at fleet scale, and binary WebTransport/MessagePack cuts egress vs. the PRD's JSON-at-30Hz. DURABILITY: excellent — Postgres append-only log + materialized projections, optional EventStoreDB, content-addressed MinIO, Temporal-resumable workflows, and the same fingerprint/dataset-hash discipline; a worker crash resumes rather than losing a multi-hour solve. MULTI-TENANCY: real but heavyweight — 'per-tenant namespace + storage isolation' on K8s plus GovCloud and an air-gapped Helm profile. That hard isolation is correct for ITAR/AS9100 customers but it is the densest-cost model (namespace-per-tenant does not pack many small tenants cheaply), and there is no soft RLS tier for that segment. COST-AT-SCALE: best per-unit compute (Rust), but the highest fixed/baseline cost of the three — a bare-metal Firecracker node pool, Temporal, NATS, a solver farm, surrogate-training pipelines, and GovCloud's price premium, with no described scale-to-zero, so utilization risk is high. COLLABORATIVE VERSIONING is the decisive weakness on this lens: event sourcing is first-class, but MERGE is simply not addressed — no CRDT, no concurrent co-editing, no answer to PRD3 §3.3 beyond AI-proposed deltas needing sign-off. Half of the named 'collaborative versioning (event sourcing + merge)' dimension is missing, which is why this sits below the plan that builds it. Net: the best raw-scale and unit-cost engine, dinged for an absent merge story and the heaviest, least-elastic cost baseline.
scores.strongest_idea: A compiled Rust/axum+tokio performance core (with cxx-bridged OCCT in a watchdogged worker) backed by Temporal for durable, backpressured solver-DAG orchestration — the combination delivers both the lowest per-request compute cost at fleet scale and resumable durability for multi-hour FEA/flutter runs.
scores.weakest_point: The 'merge' half of collaborative versioning is entirely unaddressed (no CRDT / no concurrent co-editing), and the cost baseline is the least elastic of the three — bare-metal Firecracker pool + Temporal + NATS + solver farm + GovCloud premium with no scale-to-zero, so idle utilization directly erodes the otherwise-excellent Rust unit economics.

scores.approach_name: Str( CloudForge ): A Cloud-Native, Event-Driven Co-Modeling Substrate with a Grounded-Solver Safety Plane
scores.rationale: This is the plan built for exactly this lens, and it is the only one that hits all five dimensions rather than four. SCALABILITY: the most elastic design — EKS + KEDA autoscaling on queue depth with separate node pools (warm microVM execution, compute-optimized CalculiX/Gmsh solvers, stateless Go gateways), SQS/Kinesis for backpressured job intake, Temporal for durable orchestration, and Aurora Serverless v2 that scales storage/compute with load. KEDA scale-on-queue (toward scale-to-zero for the expensive solver pool) is the best match for the spiky, heterogeneous workload this product has. MULTI-TENANCY: the strongest and only properly first-class model — PostgreSQL row-level security for dense soft-multi-tenancy, pgvector for per-project retrieval, per-tenant data residency, AND an on-prem/air-gapped Helm chart for ITAR/EAR; it is the only plan offering both cost-efficient many-tenant packing and hard-isolation when required. COLLABORATIVE VERSIONING — the dimension the other two skip — is its defining advantage: a Yjs/Automerge CRDT doc bound to the ledger gives real concurrent multi-user bounded-slider editing with presence/awareness, layered on EventStoreDB (purpose-built append-only with native streaming projections) for the event-sourcing half; crucially it makes the RIGHT safety call by downgrading semantic auto-merge of flight geometry from silent mutation to AI-PROPOSED-with-mandatory-human-review, satisfying PRD3 §3.3 without the danger of last-writer-wins on safety-critical geometry. DURABILITY: on par with Anvil and arguably ahead — EventStoreDB or managed Kinesis+DynamoDB, S3 content-addressing, Temporal-resumable DAGs, and the same fingerprint/dataset-hash replay. COST-AT-SCALE: the best trajectory for a variable-load SaaS — scale-to-zero solver pools and Aurora Serverless mean you stop paying for idle, and a Go gateway is far cheaper than Python on the hot fan-out path (though not as cheap as Anvil's Rust); the offset is managed-service premium (EventStoreDB/Kinesis, Aurora Serverless, Temporal Cloud, ElastiCache) and pgvector overhead. The one caveat keeping this off a 9: binding a CRDT directly to a ledger with cross-field invariants (bounds[0]<=value<=bounds[1], HARD_LOCK, FS>=1.5 coupling) is genuinely hard — CRDT convergence guarantees structural eventual consistency but NOT preservation of semantic/safety invariants, so concurrent valid edits can merge into an invariant-violating state; the plan mitigates this for geometry (human review) but binding Yjs to the safety scalars still needs invariant-aware merge resolvers it does not fully specify. Even so, it is strictly more than either competitor offers on collaborative merge, with the best multi-tenancy and best cost elasticity. Net: the clear leader for scale, tenancy, merge, and cost-at-scale.
scores.strongest_idea: Yjs/Automerge CRDT convergence on the ledger for true concurrent editing, deliberately combined with downgrading semantic geometry auto-merge to AI-PROPOSED-with-human-review on top of EventStoreDB streaming projections — it is the only plan that actually delivers the 'event sourcing + merge' the lens demands, and does so without the safety hazard of silent last-writer-wins on flight geometry.
scores.weakest_point: Binding a CRDT directly to a safety-critical ledger is the unfinished risk: Yjs converges the data structure but does not preserve cross-field invariants (bounds, HARD_LOCK, FS coupling), so concurrent edits can merge into an invalid state, and invariant-aware merge resolvers are not specified; secondary risk is managed-service cost premium (EventStoreDB/Kinesis + Aurora Serverless + Temporal + ElastiCache) eroding the scale-to-zero savings.

competing_plans.name: Grounded Monolith: A Python-First Co-Modeling Core with an Out-of-Band Truth Plane
competing_plans.philosophy: Fastest path to a working MVP with the fewest moving parts, while refusing to ship the three things that make the PRD dangerous rather than merely incomplete: an LLM originating safety scalars, generated code running unsandboxed, and a single clock over heterogeneous tools. The product is Python-centric because build123d is Python, so the kernel, the templated code generator, the analytic telemetry, and the solver drivers all live in one FastAPI process tree and one repo. The cuts I make to stay small are honest cuts: I relax the headline numbers (150ms applies to a graphical preview + analytic HUD, NOT to B-rep regen/FEA/slicing), I invert codegen so the LLM never emits free Python, and I push every solver and every LLM call off the interactive path into an async worker. The organizing principle is two planes: an interactive plane that is fast because it only does cheap, deterministic, closed-form work, and a truth plane (kernel regen, FEA, flutter, slicing, optimization) that is slow, grounded, content-addressed, and never blocks a frame. Minimum moving parts means Postgres is also the event store and the job-state store, MinIO is the only blob store, Redis is the only broker, and there is exactly one deployable backend image. Decisiveness over optionality: one model vendor on the hot conversational turn, one FEA solver, one slicer, one mesher.
competing_plans.mvp: 12-16 weeks to a credible MVP, re-sequenced from the PRD's plan. Weeks 1-3: FastAPI monolith + Postgres event store + hardened ledger schema (Pydantic v2, additionalProperties:false, ParameterDef $defs, round-trip verifier) + build123d pipeline in an out-of-process worker with BRepCheck/ShapeFix and the persistent feature-tag layer. Weeks 4-6: inverted codegen (Jinja2 templates + Pydantic deltas), Anthropic tool-use agents off the hot path, analytic telemetry (BOM/material DB/datum frame seeded), Firecracker/gVisor sandbox pool. Weeks 7-9: React/r3f split-pane viewport, WebSocket two-plane protocol, morph-target slider preview, 30Hz analytic HUD, human-in-the-loop review/diff surface. Weeks 10-12: Gmsh+CalculiX FS/buckling/modal + AVL/XFOIL wired as async jobs with first surrogates, PrusaSlicer CLI sidecar (AGPL-isolated) + real slice caching, NSGA-II/qEHVI over surrogates. Weeks 13-16 buffer: p-k flutter, segmented-assembly joint FEA, on-prem/vLLM SKU, export-control + PE-sign-off gating, OTel observability and per-design cost accounting. The relaxed targets and inverted codegen are what make this timeline real instead of aspirational.

competing_plans.name: Anvil — A Polyglot Performance-Core Architecture for Grounded Text-to-CAD
competing_plans.philosophy: The PRDs fail because they put one clock over four different physical processes and put a stochastic LLM inside hard-real-time and safety-critical loops. Anvil inverts both. (1) Separate the clocks: a compiled Rust/C++ core owns geometry, meshing, validity, and analytic telemetry; LLMs and real solvers (FEA/aero/flutter/slicer) live entirely off the interactive path on a durable async job queue. (2) Demote the LLM from author to proposer: it never emits free-form Python and never originates a safety scalar. It emits schema-validated parameter deltas; a deterministic Jinja+build123d templater generates the script; real grounded solvers produce every FS/stall/flutter/range number. (3) Make the ledger load-bearing, not decorative: additionalProperties:false everywhere, one reusable ParameterDef, a post-compile OCC round-trip + BRepCheck gate, and content-addressed derived-artifact events so replay folds over stored facts and never re-invokes a non-deterministic producer. The result is an honest tool that says "unknown" instead of hallucinating assurance on flight hardware.
competing_plans.mvp: The 12-week plan is not credible for anything touching flight-safety numbers. Realistic: ~9-12 months to a defensible prototype-grade MVP. Roughly: Months 1-2 — Rust core + OCCT regen + hardened ledger schema (ParameterDef, round-trip verifier, BRepCheck gate) + Postgres event log with toolchain fingerprinting. Months 3-4 — Firecracker sandbox + Jinja/build123d templated codegen + parameter-delta LLM (no free code) + WebTransport gateway. Months 4-6 — async solver farm (Gmsh/CalculiX FS+buckling+modal, AVL/XFOIL) + analytic telemetry + segmented-assembly/joint model + BOM/datum/coordinate-frame additions to the schema. Months 6-8 — surrogate training pipeline (Kriging/POD with validity domains) + real NSGA-II/qEHVI optimizer + slicer sidecar + isolation/legal review. Months 8-12 — React/r3f viewport, mesh-family drag interpolation, human-in-the-loop review/diff/accept-reject, audit/traceability + requirements matrix, hardening. Flutter/CFD/full certification-grade traceability extend beyond 12 months.

competing_plans.name: Str( CloudForge ): A Cloud-Native, Event-Driven Co-Modeling Substrate with a Grounded-Solver Safety Plane
competing_plans.philosophy: Two non-negotiable inversions fix the PRDs' fatal architecture. (1) Invert codegen: the LLM never emits free-form Python and never originates a safety scalar. It emits schema-validated parameter deltas; a deterministic Jinja2+build123d template layer produces the script; real solvers (CalculiX/AVL/XFOIL) produce every FS/stall/flutter/range number. The "single source of truth" becomes load-bearing because a post-compile OCCT round-trip verifier asserts the geometry matches the ledger before anything downstream consumes it. (2) Split one mythical clock into three honest tiers tied to the speed of the slowest tool in each: a ~16-33ms analytic HUD tier (closed-form mass/CG/print-time proxies, no kernel), a kernel-regen tier on slider-release (OCCT seconds, async), and a batch solver/optimizer tier (FEA/CFD/Pareto, minutes, off-interactive). Cloud-native managed services carry durability, multi-tenancy, and long-running compute; CRDTs carry collaborative ledger convergence; microVMs carry the arbitrary-code blast radius. The LLM lives only on the human-timescale conversational turn, never inside 150ms/30Hz/15s loops or safety verdicts.
competing_plans.mvp: ~9-12 months to a defensible MVP (the PRD's 12 weeks is only achievable for the unsafe toy). Phase A (months 1-3): hardened schema + CRDT ledger + EventStoreDB facts/derivations split; Jinja2+build123d deterministic codegen behind Firecracker pool; OCCT round-trip verifier + BRepCheck gate; analytic HUD proxies + Go WebSocket gateway; positioned-BOM + material-property DB (the missing mass model). Phase B (months 3-6): grounded solver DAG on Temporal (Gmsh+CalculiX FS/buckling/modal, AVL/XFOIL aero, propulsion/battery range with altitude derating), segmented-assembly joint modeling, datum/MAC reference frame, requirements/verification matrix. Phase C (months 6-9): offline surrogate training + real NSGA-II/qEHVI optimizer with buckling/flutter constraints; DLM p-k flutter; orientation optimization + supportless chamfer/teardrop manufacturing (drop spiral-vase for structural skins); kinematic/swept-volume gear-retraction checks. Phase D (months 9-12): React/Three.js split-pane UI, anchored HUD via TNaming IDs, human-in-the-loop review/diff/accept gate, on-prem/air-gapped + ITAR packaging, AS9100 audit, IP/export legal sign-off. A clickable demo (proxy preview + analytic HUD + conversational deltas, no real solvers) is reachable in ~6 weeks, but it must be labeled non-engineering.
